coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as effective probes for label-free fluorescence detection of dopamine
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jingjin
Zhao, Limin
Lan, Chuanqing
Zhao, Shulin
coredata.dc:description: A label-free fluorescence based method using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as effective probes was developed for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine (DA). The initial strong blue fluorescence of the GQDs in aqueous solution was effectively quenched upon addition of DA. The quenching mechanism may involve transfer of electrons from the photoexcited GQDs to dopamine–quinine, which was produced via the oxidization of DA by ambient O2 in alkaline solution. The quenching efficiency was linearly proportional to the concentration of DA within the range of 0.25–50μM with a low detection limit down of 0.09μM. The proposed sensing system is simple and low-cost with facile experimental operations, and has a high selectivity for DA over a number of possible interfering species. Additionally, this method was successfully applied to the determination of DA in biological samples with satisfactory recoveries (98.8–106.4%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence probe
Label-free method
Dopamine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of GQDs. Inset: photographs of the solution of GQDs taken under visible light (left) and under 367nm UV light (right).
(a) FTIR spectra of GQDs. (b) The AFM image of GQDs on mica substrates with the height profile along the line in the image.
The fluorescence spectra of 0.14mg/mL GQDs in the absence and presence of 50μM DA. Inset: photographs of GQDs solutions with or without DA taken under 367nm UV light.
(a) Fluorescence response of the proposed method at different pH values. (b) Time-dependent fluorescence response of the proposed method. GQDs: 0.14mg/mL, DA: 50μM.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system after addition of various concentrations of DA (a–i: 0, 0.25, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50 and 100μM). Inset: The relationship between the florescence quenching and the concentration of DA (0.25, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50μM).
The fluorescence response of GQDs (0.14mg/mL) in the presence of 50μM different species.
Schematic illustration of the developed graphene quantum dots based fluorescence sensing system for DA detection.
The comparison of the proposed method with other reported methods of dopamine determination.

row.entry: CdSe QDs based anodic electrochemiluminescence sensor
Graphene QDs based lable-free fluorescent sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of DA in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as effective probes for label-free fluorescence detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Resource, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Resource, Guangxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources of Education Ministry, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources of Education Ministry, Guangxi Normal University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Environmental Science and Resource, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Resource, Guangxi Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A label-free fluorescence based method using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as effective probes was developed for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine (DA). The initial strong blue fluorescence of the GQDs in aqueous solution was effectively quenched upon addition of DA. The quenching mechanism may involve transfer of electrons from the photoexcited GQDs to dopamine–quinine, which was produced via the oxidization of DA by ambient O2 in alkaline solution. The quenching efficiency was linearly proportional to the concentration of DA within the range of 0.25–50μM with a low detection limit down of 0.09μM. The proposed sensing system is simple and low-cost with facile experimental operations, and has a high selectivity for DA over a number of possible interfering species. Additionally, this method was successfully applied to the determination of DA in biological samples with satisfactory recoveries (98.8–106.4%).

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new member of the graphene family, were discovered very recently as a class of zero-dimensional graphitic nanomaterials with lateral dimensions less than 100nm in single layer, double layers, and a few layers [1–4]. GQDs are superior in terms of chemical inertness, ease of production, resistance to photo-bleaching, and exhibit low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility in comparison with conventional semiconductor QDs. Moreover, similar to graphene, GQDs have large surface areas, large diameters, and offer better surface grafting using the π–π conjugated network or surface groups. Notably, due to the quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs exhibit high photoluminescence and slow hot-carrier relaxation, making their properties distinct from those of conventional graphene sheets [5,6]. These merits of GQDs make them promising in catalytic reactions, drug delivery systems, sensors and bioimaging [7–10]. Although some applications of GQDs in photovoltaic devices and electrochemical catalysis have been reported [11,12], the obstacles of their application as optical probes originate mainly from the difficulties in constructing functionalized GQDs that can selectively recognize targets and simultaneously produce a sensitive signal response.

Dopamine (DA) is a neurotransmitter that plays an essential role in the function of the central nervous and cardiovascular systems [13,14]. Several important nervous system diseases are associated with dysfunctions of DA, including Parkinson's disease [15]. In light of the significance of dopamine and its detection, many versatile assay methods for assay of dopamine have been developed, such as electrochemical [16], chemiluminescence [17], high-performance liquid chromatography [18], capillary electrophoresis [19] and spectroscopic approaches [20]. Because DA can be easily oxidized, electrochemically techniques for DA detection have attracted considerable interest in recent years [21–23]. However, some biochemical compounds such as ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA), the levels of which are 100–1000 times higher than that of DA, are oxidized at nearly the same potential. Overlap of the voltammetric responses of these compounds makes the sensitive and selective detection of DA highly difficult [24]. To overcome this problem, various modified electrodes have been constructed [25–27]. Although these electrodes have been used to distinguish the overlapped peaks to some extent, and selective or simultaneous determination of DA has been achieved, the process required for electrode modification is still complex and time-consuming. Hence, more attention has recently been paid to fluorescent approaches due to the simplicity and low detection limit. Nevertheless, detection of DA in biological matrixes generally suffers from a series of interferences caused by other aromatic acids and amino acids, particularly AA and UA. Therefore, the development of facile, low-cost, sensitive, and selective fluorescence-based methods for the detection of DA remains an urgent task.

In this study, we prepare water-soluble and blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and develop a label-free fluorescence-based assay for sensitive and selective detection of DA based on GQDs. It is reported that DA, as a type of hydroquinone, can be easily oxidized by ambient molecular O2 at basic pH, generating dopamine–quinine, which is a known electron acceptor and can induce fluorescence quenching of QDs through the electron transfer process [28–31]. Herein, we speculate that DA can also quench the fluorescence of GQDs, similar to the fluorescence quenching of QDs. Based on this mechanism, a sensitive method for DA detection is developed by recording the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. This sensing system is practically facile, low-cost, and exhibits adequate performance for biological samples.

Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Co. Inc. (Heysham, UK). l-ascorbic acid (AA) and human serum albumin (HSA) were bought from Sigma–Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). d-tryptophan (d-Try), dl-threonine (dl-Thr), l-α-alanine (l-α-Ala), l-histidine (l-His), d-serine (d-Ser), l-lysine (l-Lys), l-leucine (l-Leu), l-phenylalanine (l-Phe) were obtained from Chinese medicine group Shanghai reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Uric acid (UA) and anilin were provided by Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Galactose (Gal), fructose (Fru), mannose (Man) were supplied by Acros Organics Co. (Geel, Belgium). Citric acid was purchased from Guangzhou chemical reagent factory (Guangzhou, China). Human serum samples were kindly provided by the No. 5 People's Hospital (Guilin, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Water was purified with a Milli-Q plus 185 equip from Millipore (Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout the work.

Absorbance measurements were performed using a TU-1901 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co, Ltd., China). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were collected with a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA JOBIN JVON, France). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) data were obtained by a Spectrum Two FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA).

The GQDs were synthesized according to the documented protocol [32]. Briefly, 2g of citric acid was pladed into a test tube and heated to 200°C, the temperature was maintained for about 20min, at which point the color of the liquid changed to orange. Under vigorous stirring, the obtained orange liquid was added dropwise to 100mL of 0.1mg/mL NaOH solution. After neutralization to 7.0 with HCl solution, the aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained.

The GQD solution (1.4mg/mL, 10μL), PBS buffer (pH 8.0, 80μL), and various concentrations of DA standard solution or sample (10μL) were sequentially added to a series of 0.5mL centrifuge tubes. The mixture was then thoroughly homogenized and incubated in a water bath for 4h at 37°C. Subsequently, the mixture was removed from the water bath and allowed to cool to room temperature for 5min for the fluorescence measurements. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 367nm and an emission wavelength of 453nm. The slits for both the excitation and the emission were set to 10nm and the photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage was 800V.

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the aqueous GQD solution (Fig. 1 ) show that the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength of the GQDs are 367 and 453nm, respectively. The GQDs exhibits a UV–vis absorption band centered around 360nm, which corresponds well to the excitation spectra. Furthermore, the colorless aqueous GQD solution emitted bright blue light when excited with a 367nm UV beam (Fig. 1, inset). The fluorescence intensity also has not obvious change after continuous excitation at 367nm UV light for 260min (Fig. S1), indicating a good fluorescence stability of GQDs.

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the aqueous GQD solution (Fig. 1) show that the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength of the GQDs are 367 and 453nm, respectively. The GQDs exhibits a UV–vis absorption band centered around 360nm, which corresponds well to the excitation spectra. Furthermore, the colorless aqueous GQD solution emitted bright blue light when excited with a 367nm UV beam (Fig. 1, inset). The fluorescence intensity also has not obvious change after continuous excitation at 367nm UV light for 260min (Fig. S1), indicating a good fluorescence stability of GQDs.

FTIR was used to characterize the groups on the surface of the obtained GQDs (Fig. 2a). The IR spectrum of the GQDs presents intense absorption features around 3429cm−1 from the OH group and 1079cm−1 from the COH group, confirming the presence of the OH group. The peaks at 1580cm−1 from the CO bond and at 1395cm−1 from the COO– groups confirm the presence of the COOH group; the peak at 2981cm−1 originates from the C-H bond. The OH and COOH groups on the surface of the GQDs impart good aqueous solubility to the GQDs. Furthermore, AFM analysis indicates that the GQDs used in this study are mostly nanosheets with a height of ∼1.5nm (Fig. 2b). And the size distribution (Fig. S2) is mostly in the range of 17–21nm according to the AFM results.

FTIR was used to characterize the groups on the surface of the obtained GQDs (Fig. 2a). The IR spectrum of the GQDs presents intense absorption features around 3429cm−1 from the OH group and 1079cm−1 from the COH group, confirming the presence of the OH group. The peaks at 1580cm−1 from the CO bond and at 1395cm−1 from the COO– groups confirm the presence of the COOH group; the peak at 2981cm−1 originates from the C-H bond. The OH and COOH groups on the surface of the GQDs impart good aqueous solubility to the GQDs. Furthermore, AFM analysis indicates that the GQDs used in this study are mostly nanosheets with a height of ∼1.5nm (Fig. 2b). And the size distribution (Fig. S2) is mostly in the range of 17–21nm according to the AFM results.

As is well known, dopamine can be oxidized by ambient O2 to generate dopamine–quinone species in alkaline solution. It was reported that the produced dopamine–quinone species exhibit a characteristic absorption around 390nm [33]. Here, we also demonstrated the generation of dopamine–quinone species by recording the UV–vis spectra before and after the incubation of DA under basic condition (Fig. S3). Previous reports have shown that dopamine–quinone is an electron acceptor for QDs [28–31]. Therefore, a possible mechanism by which fluorescence quenching occurs in the proposed fluorescent sensing strategy for the detection of DA is presented in Scheme 1 . The GQD solution exhibited strong fluorescence intensity (FL) in the absence of DA. The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the GQDs enable noncovalent interaction with diols, amine functional groups, and phenyl in DA through electrostatic inte interactions, π–π stacking, and hydrogen bonding. Under alkaline conditions, DA is oxidized to dopamine–quinone that can accept electrons from the photoexcited GQDs with consequent fluorescence quenching.

As is well known, dopamine can be oxidized by ambient O2 to generate dopamine–quinone species in alkaline solution. It was reported that the produced dopamine–quinone species exhibit a characteristic absorption around 390nm [33]. Here, we also demonstrated the generation of dopamine–quinone species by recording the UV–vis spectra before and after the incubation of DA under basic condition (Fig. S3). Previous reports have shown that dopamine–quinone is an electron acceptor for QDs [28–31]. Therefore, a possible mechanism by which fluorescence quenching occurs in the proposed fluorescent sensing strategy for the detection of DA is presented in Scheme 1. The GQD solution exhibited strong fluorescence intensity (FL) in the absence of DA. The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the GQDs enable noncovalent interaction with diols, amine functional groups, and phenyl in DA through electrostatic inte interactions, π–π stacking, and hydrogen bonding. Under alkaline conditions, DA is oxidized to dopamine–quinone that can accept electrons from the photoexcited GQDs with consequent fluorescence quenching.

The time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the GQDs in the absence or presence of DA was measured to further investigate the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence decay curves are shown in Fig. S4 and the fluorescence lifetimes are listed in Table S1. In the absence and presence of DA, the fluorescence lifetimes of the GQDs were almost the same, implying that the quenching mechanism involves static quenching rather than dynamic quenching [34,35].

The time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the GQDs in the absence or presence of DA was measured to further investigate the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence decay curves are shown in Fig. S4 and the fluorescence lifetimes are listed in Table S1. In the absence and presence of DA, the fluorescence lifetimes of the GQDs were almost the same, implying that the quenching mechanism involves static quenching rather than dynamic quenching [34,35].

The feasibility of the strategy for DA detection based on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 , the GQDs emitted strongly at 453nm in the absence of DA, and the fluorescence intensity decreased by 79.2% after addition of 50μM DA to the system. This observation may be attributed to complexation of DA with the GQDs.

The pH of the buffer is a key factor for the sensing system, since the pH may affect the fluorescence of the GQDs and the generation of dopamine–quinone. Fig. 4a shows the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs in the pH range of 6.0–9.0. The fluorescence intensity of the GQDs was found to be pH dependent, and the fluorescence remained essentially stable in the pH range of 7.0–9.0 but decreased below pH 7.0. In addition, fluorescence quenching was enhanced at higher pH, whereas the fluorescence intensity remained constant above pH 8.0, which is consistent with the fact that the generation of dopamine–quinone is favored under basic conditions. The experimental results show that the maximal response of the system was obtained at pH 8.0, and this pH was selected as the optimum pH for the buffer solution. The incubation time of the sensing system was also optimized, as shown in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence intensity at 453nm declined with time and changed slowly after 4h. In order to shorten the analysis time, the incubation time of 4h was chosen for further applications.

The analytical performance of the developed system was investigated by analysis of various concentrations of DA under the optimized assay conditions. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence spectra of the GQDs in the presence of different concentrations of DA. The fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing DA concentration. A calibration graph was constructed by plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio F
0 /F (where F 0 and F are respectively the fluorescence intensity of the system at 453nm in the absence and presence of the target) versus the concentration of DA (Fig. 5, inset). A good linear relationship was achieved in the DA concentration range of 0.25–50.0μM. The regression equation is F
0 /F =0.0729C +1.0394, where C is the concentration of DA (μM), with a correlation coefficient of 0.9958. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.09μM according to the rule that the LOD is three times the standard deviation. Compared with other reported literatures [16,20,36–40], the graphene QDs based lable-free fluorescent sensor proposed in this work gives a relatively lower limit of detection and wider linear range (listed in Table 1 ).

To evaluate the specificity of the present method, selective detection of DA relative to several species such as HSA, urea, AA, aniline, sugars, and several amino acids was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6 , fluorescence quenching of GQD was only induced by the addition of DA, and none of the other species led to obvious changes in the fluorescence intensity at the same concentration as DA. Notably, despite the similarity of the electrochemical properties of AA and UA to those of DA, AA and UA did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs. These results clearly demonstrate the high specificity of the developed fluorescence sensing system for DA.

To demonstrate the practical feasibility of designed method, target analytes in real samples (healthy human serum) were assayed. The serum sample diluted 200-fold was spiked with 2.5μM, 5μM, and 10μM of DA and the concentration of DA in the samples was determined by the standard additions method. As shown in Table 2 , satisfactory recoveries of DA in human serum were obtained. Thus, the proposed sensing system may hold potential application for DA detection in biological samples with a quick and simple manner.

In summary, a label-free fluorescence-based method exploiting luminescent graphene quantum dots was developed and applied to the sensitive and selective detection of dopamine. The fluorescence of the GQDs was effectively quenched by DA. A possible mechanism for this fluorescence quenching involves electron transfer from the GQDs to dopamine–quinine species generated by the oxidization of DA under alkaline conditions. The proposed sensing system has several features. First, the method is simple and low-cost with facile experimental protocols. Second, the assay offers high sensitivity and specificity without requiring modification processes. In addition, the synthesized GQDs are minimally toxic and should be advantageous over CdTe quantum dots for in vivo applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305020, 21175030), Natural Science Foundations of Guangxi Province (2014GXNSFBA118041) and Guangxi Normal University (2013ZD006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.09.105.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

Direct functionalization of nanodiamond particles using dopamine derivatives

Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer-based detection for microchip electrophoresis

Aptamer-based colorimetric biosensing of dopamine using unmodified gold nanoparticles
The electrochemical modification of clenbuterol for biosensors of dopamine, norepinephrine, adrenalin, ascorbic acid and uric acid at paraffin-impregnated graphite electrode
Electrochemical detection of dopamine using arrays of liquid-liquid micro-interfaces created within micromachined silicon membranes
In vivo electrochemical measurements of exogenously applied dopamine in Drosophila melanogaster
Amplified voltammetric detection of dopamine using ferrocene-capped gold nanoparticle/streptavidin conjugates

Au nanoparticles decorated polypyrrole/reduced graphene oxide hybrid sheets for ultrasensitive dopamine detection
Quantum-dot/dopamine bioconjugates function as redox coupled assemblies for in vitro and intracellular pH sensing
On the pH-dependent quenching of quantum dot photoluminescence by redox active dopamine

Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Autoxidation of dopamine: a comparison of luminescent and spectrophotometric detection in basic solutions
A novel one-pot route for large-scale preparation of highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots powders

Selective sensing of dopamine at MnOOH nanobelt modified electrode
Graphene oxide based photoinduced charge transfer label-free near-infrared fluorescent biosensor for dopamine

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jingjin Zhao received both her M.S. and Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from University of Hunan in 2009 and 2012, respectively. Now, she works in Guangxi Normal University and majors in chemical and biological sensing technology.
Limin Zhao received her M.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Guangxi Normal University in 2014.
Chuanqing Lan received his M.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Guangxi Normal University in 2015.

Shulin Zhao is a professor. He received his B.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Hunan University, and Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering from Sichuan University. Now, he works in Guangxi Normal University and majors in capillary electrophoresis, and chemical and biological sensing technology. He has published over 300 research papers.
